abstract: This article examines the pressing need to reform organizational and management mechanisms within higher education institutions to better meet the demands of the modern socio-economic landscape. The author highlights the transition from centralized control toward institutional autonomy, focusing on the implementation of academic and financial independence as catalysts for healthy competition and innovation. By analyzing various international management models—including liberal and centrist approaches—the study contextualizes Uzbekistan’s current educational reforms, such as the adoption of credit-module systems and the enhancement of public-private partnerships. Central to the discussion is the role of management culture, leadership ethics, and the integration of both socio-psychological and rational-economic factors in boosting institutional efficiency. The findings emphasize that modernizing management principles is essential for producing competitive, highly qualified specialists capable of contributing to national development and achieving international recognition in global university rankings.
